AI.conclusion: WaterSIC demonstrates superior quantization performance by adapting classical information theory principles to neural network compression, significantly outperforming existing methods like GPTQ and achieving near-optimal compression rates.

title: GCAgent: Enhancing Group Chat Communication through Dialogue Agents System
summary: As a key form in online social platforms, group chat is a popular space for interest exchange or problem-solving, but its effectiveness is often hindered by inactivity and management challenges. While recent large language models (LLMs) have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their seamlessly integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored. To address this gap, we introduce GCAgent, an LLM-driven system for enhancing group chats communication with both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents. The system comprises three tightly integrated modules: Agent Builder, which customizes agents to align with users' interests; Dialogue Manager, which coordinates dialogue states and manage agent invocations; and Interface Plugins, which reduce interaction barriers by three distinct tools. Through extensive experiment, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04\% of cases compared to its base model. Additionally, in real-world deployments over 350 days, it increased message volume by 28.80\%, significantly improving group activity and engagement. Overall, this work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agent from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios.

AI.tldr: GCAgent is an LLM-driven system that enhances group chats by integrating entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents into multi-participant conversations, overcoming inactivity and management challenges that plague online group discussions.
AI.motivation: Group chats are popular for interest exchange and problem-solving but suffer from inactivity and management challenges. While LLMs have powered impressive one-to-one conversational agents, their integration into multi-participant conversations remains unexplored, creating a gap in enhancing group chat effectiveness.
AI.method: GCAgent comprises three integrated modules: 1) Agent Builder customizes agents to align with users' interests, 2) Dialogue Manager coordinates dialogue states and manages agent invocations, and 3) Interface Plugins with three distinct tools to reduce interaction barriers. The system leverages LLMs to create both entertainment- and utility-oriented dialogue agents.
AI.result: In experiments, GCAgent achieved an average score of 4.68 across various criteria and was preferred in 51.04% of cases compared to its base model. Real-world deployments over 350 days showed a 28.80% increase in message volume, significantly improving group activity and engagement.
AI.conclusion: This work presents a practical blueprint for extending LLM-based dialogue agents from one-party chats to multi-party group scenarios, demonstrating that LLM-driven systems can effectively enhance group chat communication through customized agents that address both entertainment and utility needs.

title: Mixture of Universal Experts: Scaling Virtual Width via Depth-Width Transformation
summary: Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) decouples model capacity from per-token computation, yet their scalability remains limited by the physical dimensions of depth and width. To overcome this, we propose Mixture of Universal Experts (MOUE),a MoE generalization introducing a novel scaling dimension: Virtual Width. In general, MoUE aims to reuse a universal layer-agnostic expert pool across layers, converting depth into virtual width under a fixed per-token activation budget. However, two challenges remain: a routing path explosion from recursive expert reuse, and a mismatch between the exposure induced by reuse and the conventional load-balancing objectives. We address these with three core components: a Staggered Rotational Topology for structured expert sharing, a Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware exposure correction, and a Universal Router with lightweight trajectory state for coherent multi-step routing. Empirically, MoUE consistently outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and reveals a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures.

AI.tldr: MOUE introduces Virtual Width as a new scaling dimension for MoE architectures by reusing a universal expert pool across layers, overcoming depth and width limitations while maintaining fixed per-token computation.
AI.motivation: Current Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) architectures are limited by physical dimensions of depth and width, restricting scalability despite their computational efficiency advantages.
AI.method: MoUE converts depth into virtual width by reusing universal, layer-agnostic expert pools across layers. It addresses routing path explosion and exposure mismatch with three components: Staggered Rotational Topology for structured sharing, Universal Expert Load Balance for depth-aware correction, and Universal Router with trajectory state for multi-step routing.
AI.result: MoUE outperforms matched MoE baselines by up to 1.3% across scaling regimes, enables progressive conversion of existing MoE checkpoints with up to 4.2% gains, and demonstrates the viability of Virtual Width as a new scaling dimension.
AI.conclusion: MoUE successfully introduces Virtual Width as a novel scaling dimension for MoE architectures, overcoming traditional depth/width limitations while improving performance and enabling backward-compatible checkpoint upgrades.

title: X-RAY: Mapping LLM Reasoning Capability via Formalized and Calibrated Probes
summary: Large language models (LLMs) achieve promising performance, yet their ability to reason remains poorly understood. Existing evaluations largely emphasize task-level accuracy, often conflating pattern matching with reasoning capability. We present X-RAY, an explainable reasoning analysis system that maps the LLM reasoning capability using calibrated, formally verified probes. We model reasoning capability as a function of extractable \textit{structure}, operationalized through formal properties such as constraint interaction, reasoning depth, and solution-space geometry. X-Ray generates probes via formal tools with controlled structural variations, enabling precise isolation of incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on problems ranging from junior-level to advanced in mathematics, physics, and chemistry. Our analysis reveals a systematic asymmetry in LLM reasoning: models are relatively robust to constraint refinement, where additional conditions shrink an existing solution space, but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring, where modifications alter the underlying structural form of the solution manifold. Moreover, calibrated formal probes differentiate models that appear indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal failure modes that are structurally interpretable rather than opaque. Beyond evaluation, our framework is contamination-free and supports the training and testing of reasoning models.

AI.tldr: X-RAY is an explainable reasoning analysis system that uses formally verified probes to map LLM reasoning capabilities by modeling them as functions of extractable structure through formal properties like constraint interaction and reasoning depth.
AI.motivation: Current LLM evaluations focus on task-level accuracy but conflate pattern matching with true reasoning capability, leaving LLMs' actual reasoning abilities poorly understood and inadequately measured.
AI.method: X-RAY generates calibrated formal probes with controlled structural variations, using formal tools to isolate incremental structural information through formal calibration and verification. It models reasoning capability as a function of extractable structure operationalized through formal properties.
AI.result: LLMs show systematic asymmetry: robust to constraint refinement (additional conditions shrinking solution space) but degrade sharply under solution-space restructuring (modifications altering structural form). Formal probes differentiate models indistinguishable on standard benchmarks and reveal structurally interpretable failure modes.
AI.conclusion: X-RAY provides a contamination-free framework for analyzing LLM reasoning capabilities through formal structural analysis, offering more precise evaluation than traditional benchmarks while supporting training/testing of reasoning models.

title: Functionality-Oriented LLM Merging on the Fisher--Rao Manifold
summary:
Weight-space merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned LLMs into a single model without retraining, yet most existing approaches remain fundamentally parameter-space heuristics. This creates three practical limitations. First, linear averaging, task vectors, and related rules operate on Euclidean coordinates, even though the desired goal is to merge functionality, i.e., predictive behaviors across tasks. Second, when the source checkpoints are farther apart or more heterogeneous, Euclidean blends often trigger representation collapse, manifested as activation variance shrinkage and effective-rank degradation, which sharply degrades accuracy. Third, many geometry-inspired methods are most natural for two-model interpolation and do not extend cleanly to merging N>2 experts with a principled objective.
  We address these issues by formulating model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher--Rao manifold, which is locally equivalent to minimizing a KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. We derive a practical fixed-point algorithm using a lightweight spherical proxy that preserves norms and generalizes directly to multi-expert merging. Across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics, our method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines.

AI.tldr: A new method for merging multiple LLMs using Fisher-Rao manifold geometry instead of Euclidean parameter averaging, addressing representation collapse and enabling principled multi-model merging.
AI.motivation: Existing weight-space merging methods are limited: they use Euclidean parameter heuristics rather than focusing on predictive behavior, suffer from representation collapse with heterogeneous models, and don't scale well to merging N>2 experts with clear objectives.
AI.method: Formulate model merging as computing a weighted Karcher mean on the Fisher-Rao manifold, which minimizes KL-based function distance between predictive distributions. Develop a practical fixed-point algorithm using lightweight spherical proxies that preserve norms and generalizes to multi-expert merging.
AI.result: The method remains stable as the number and heterogeneity of merged models increase, consistently outperforming prior baselines across various benchmarks and collapse diagnostics.
AI.conclusion: Fisher-Rao manifold geometry provides a principled solution to LLM merging that addresses fundamental limitations of Euclidean approaches, enabling stable and effective combination of multiple heterogeneous models while preserving functional behavior.

title: STRUCTUREDAGENT: Planning with AND/OR Trees for Long-Horizon Web Tasks
summary: Recent advances in large language models (LLMs) have enabled agentic systems for sequential decision-making. Such agents must perceive their environment, reason across multiple time steps, and take actions that optimize long-term objectives. However, existing web agents struggle on complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination. To address these challenges, we propose STRUCTUREDAGENT, a hierarchical planning framework with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner that uses dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks. The framework also produces interpretable hierarchical plans, enabling easier debugging and facilitating human intervention when needed. Our results on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks show that STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents.

AI.tldr: STRUCTUREDAGENT is a hierarchical planning framework for LLM-based web agents that addresses challenges in long-horizon tasks through hierarchical planning with AND/OR trees and structured memory for improved constraint satisfaction.
AI.motivation: Existing web agents struggle with complex, long-horizon tasks due to limited in-context memory for tracking history, weak planning abilities, and greedy behaviors that lead to premature termination.
AI.method: Proposes STRUCTUREDAGENT with two core components: (1) an online hierarchical planner using dynamic AND/OR trees for efficient search, and (2) a structured memory module that tracks and maintains candidate solutions to improve constraint satisfaction in information-seeking tasks.
AI.result: STRUCTUREDAGENT improves performance on long-horizon web-browsing tasks compared to standard LLM-based agents, as shown on WebVoyager, WebArena, and custom shopping benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The hierarchical planning framework with structured memory enables LLM-based agents to better handle long-horizon web tasks while providing interpretable hierarchical plans for easier debugging and human intervention.

title: Lightweight and Scalable Transfer Learning Framework for Load Disaggregation
comment: This work has been submitted to the IEEE for possible publication
summary: Non-Intrusive Load Monitoring (NILM) aims to estimate appliance-level consumption from aggregate electrical signals recorded at a single measurement point. In recent years, the field has increasingly adopted deep learning approaches; however, cross-domain generalization remains a persistent challenge due to variations in appliance characteristics, usage patterns, and background loads across homes. Transfer learning provides a practical paradigm to adapt models with limited target data. However, existing methods often assume a fixed appliance set, lack flexibility for evolving real-world deployments, remain unsuitable for edge devices, or scale poorly for real-time operation. This paper proposes RefQuery, a scalable multi-appliance, multi-task NILM framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints, allowing one shared model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set. RefQuery keeps a pretrained disaggregation network fully frozen and adapts to a target home by learning only a per-appliance embedding during a lightweight backpropagation stage. Experiments on three public datasets demonstrate that RefQuery delivers a strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines, including modern Transformer-based methods. These results support RefQuery as a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices.

AI.tldr: RefQuery is a scalable multi-appliance NILM framework that uses compact appliance fingerprints and per-appliance embeddings to enable one model to serve many appliances without a fixed output set, achieving good accuracy-efficiency trade-off for edge deployment.
AI.motivation: Deep learning NILM methods struggle with cross-domain generalization due to appliance variations across homes. Existing transfer learning approaches have limitations: fixed appliance sets, lack of flexibility for evolving deployments, poor scalability for edge devices, and inability to operate in real-time.
AI.method: RefQuery is a multi-appliance, multi-task framework that conditions disaggregation on compact appliance fingerprints. It keeps a pretrained disaggregation network frozen and adapts to target homes by learning only per-appliance embeddings during lightweight backpropagation, enabling one shared model to serve many appliances.
AI.result: Experiments on three public datasets show RefQuery delivers strong accuracy-efficiency trade-off against single-appliance and multi-appliance baselines (including modern Transformer-based methods).
AI.conclusion: RefQuery provides a practical path toward scalable, real-time NILM on resource-constrained edge devices by addressing key limitations of existing approaches while maintaining good performance.

title: WebChain: A Large-Scale Human-Annotated Dataset of Real-World Web Interaction Traces
summary: We introduce WebChain, the largest open-source dataset of human-annotated trajectories on real-world websites, designed to accelerate reproducible research in web agents. It contains 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring a core Triple Alignment of visual, structural, and action data to provide rich, multi-modal supervision. The data is collected via a scalable pipeline that ensures coverage of complex, high-value tasks often missed by synthetic methods. Leveraging this dataset, we propose a Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning, achieving state-of-the-art performance on our proposed WebChainBench and other public GUI benchmarks. Our work provides the data and insights necessary to build and rigorously evaluate the next generation of scalable web agents.

AI.tldr: WebChain is a large-scale dataset of human-annotated web interaction trajectories with 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps, featuring multi-modal alignment, plus a Dual Mid-Training method that achieves SOTA performance on web agent benchmarks.
AI.motivation: The need for large-scale, high-quality datasets for reproducible web agent research, as existing synthetic methods miss complex real-world tasks and lack multi-modal supervision needed for robust web agents.
AI.method: 1) Created WebChain dataset through scalable pipeline collecting human-annotated trajectories on real websites with Triple Alignment (visual, structural, action data), 2) Proposed Dual Mid-Training recipe that decouples spatial grounding from planning for better learning.
AI.result: Achieved state-of-the-art performance on both WebChainBench (proposed benchmark) and other public GUI benchmarks. The dataset provides 31,725 trajectories and 318k steps with rich multi-modal supervision.
AI.conclusion: WebChain provides essential data and insights for building and rigorously evaluating scalable web agents, with the Dual Mid-Training approach demonstrating significant performance improvements on web agent benchmarks.

title: UniSTOK: Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging
summary: Spatio-temporal kriging aims to infer signals at unobserved locations from observed sensors and is critical to applications such as transportation and environmental monitoring. In practice, however, observed sensors themselves often exhibit heterogeneous missingness, forcing inductive kriging models to rely on crudely imputed inputs. This setting brings three key challenges: (1) it is unclear whether an value is a true signal or a missingness-induced artifact; (2) missingness is highly heterogeneous across sensors and time; (3) missing observations distort the local spatio-temporal structure. To address these issues, we propose Uniform Inductive Spatio-Temporal Kriging (UniSTOK), a plug-and-play framework that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones under missing observation. Our framework forms a dual-branch input consisting of the original observations and a jigsaw-augmented counterpart that synthesizes proxy signals only at missing entries. The two branches are then processed in parallel by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation. Their outputs are finally adaptively fused via dual-channel attention. Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements.

AI.tldr: UniSTOK is a plug-and-play framework that enhances inductive spatio-temporal kriging models by handling heterogeneous missing data through dual-branch processing with jigsaw augmentation and mask modulation.
AI.motivation: Real-world spatio-temporal kriging faces challenges when sensor data has heterogeneous missingness, which leads to three key issues: ambiguity between true signals and missingness artifacts, high heterogeneity in missing patterns across sensors and time, and distortion of local spatio-temporal structure due to missing observations.
AI.method: UniSTOK uses a dual-branch approach: one branch processes original observations while another processes jigsaw-augmented counterparts that synthesize proxy signals only at missing entries. Both branches are processed by a shared spatio-temporal backbone with explicit missingness mask modulation, and outputs are fused via dual-channel attention.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world datasets under diverse missing patterns demonstrate consistent and significant improvements in spatio-temporal kriging performance compared to existing methods.
AI.conclusion: UniSTOK effectively addresses the challenges of heterogeneous missing data in spatio-temporal kriging through its innovative dual-branch architecture with jigsaw augmentation and mask modulation, providing a plug-and-play solution that enhances existing inductive kriging backbones.

title: Non-Euclidean Gradient Descent Operates at the Edge of Stability
summary: The Edge of Stability (EoS) is a phenomenon where the sharpness (largest eigenvalue) of the Hessian converges to $2/η$ during training with gradient descent (GD) with a step-size $η$. Despite (apparently) violating classical smoothness assumptions, EoS has been widely observed in deep learning, but its theoretical foundations remain incomplete. We provide an interpretation of EoS through the lens of Directional Smoothness Mishkin et al. [2024]. This interpretation naturally extends to non-Euclidean norms, which we use to define generalized sharpness under an arbitrary norm. Our generalized sharpness measure includes previously studied vanilla GD and preconditioned GD as special cases, as well as methods for which EoS has not been studied, such as $\ell_{\infty}$-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum. Through experiments on neural networks, we show that non-Euclidean GD with our generalized sharpness also exhibits progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around or above the threshold $2/η$. Practically, our framework provides a single, geometry-aware spectral measure that works across optimizers.

AI.tldr: The paper extends the Edge of Stability (EoS) phenomenon beyond Euclidean GD to non-Euclidean optimization methods using Directional Smoothness, showing that various optimizers exhibit similar progressive sharpening and oscillation patterns around threshold 2/η.
AI.motivation: The Edge of Stability phenomenon violates classical smoothness assumptions but is widely observed in deep learning, yet theoretical foundations remain incomplete. Current understanding is limited to vanilla gradient descent.
AI.method: Extends EoS through Directional Smoothness lens to non-Euclidean norms, defining generalized sharpness under arbitrary norms. Framework includes vanilla GD, preconditioned GD, ℓ∞-descent, Block CD, Spectral GD, and Muon without momentum as special cases.
AI.result: Non-Euclidean GD with generalized sharpness also exhibits progressive sharpening followed by oscillations around/above threshold 2/η. Provides unified geometry-aware spectral measure across optimizers.
AI.conclusion: EoS is not specific to Euclidean GD but general phenomenon across non-Euclidean optimization methods, unified under directional smoothness framework with practical single spectral measure.

title: Building AI Coding Agents for the Terminal: Scaffolding, Harness, Context Engineering, and Lessons Learned
comment: Work in progress, new versions will be updated continuously
summary: The landscape of AI coding assistance is undergoing a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to versatile, terminal-native agents. Operating directly where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments, CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks. In this paper, we present OPENDEV, an open-source, command-line coding agent engineered specifically for this new paradigm. Effective autonomous assistance requires strict safety controls and highly efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation. OPENDEV overcomes these challenges through a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, and adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations. Furthermore, it employs an automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions and counteracts instruction fade-out through event-driven system reminders. By enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.

AI.tldr: OPENDEV is an open-source CLI-based AI coding agent that operates directly in terminals, using a compound AI system with specialized model routing, dual-agent architecture, and adaptive context management to enable safe, efficient autonomous software development.
AI.motivation: There's a fundamental shift from complex IDE plugins to terminal-native AI coding agents that can operate where developers manage source control, execute builds, and deploy environments. CLI-based agents offer unprecedented autonomy for long-horizon development tasks but require strict safety controls and efficient context management to prevent context bloat and reasoning degradation.
AI.method: OPENDEV uses a compound AI system architecture with workload-specialized model routing, a dual-agent architecture separating planning from execution, lazy tool discovery, adaptive context compaction that progressively reduces older observations, automated memory system to accumulate project-specific knowledge across sessions, and event-driven system reminders to counteract instruction fade-out.
AI.result: OPENDEV provides a secure, extensible foundation for terminal-first AI assistance by enforcing explicit reasoning phases and prioritizing context efficiency, offering a blueprint for robust autonomous software engineering.
AI.conclusion: OPENDEV represents a new paradigm in AI coding assistance, shifting from IDE plugins to terminal-native agents that operate where developers work, with architectural innovations that address key challenges in safety, context management, and autonomous software development.

title: Poisoning the Inner Prediction Logic of Graph Neural Networks for Clean-Label Backdoor Attacks
summary: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) have achieved remarkable results in various tasks. Recent studies reveal that graph backdoor attacks can poison the GNN model to predict test nodes with triggers attached as the target class. However, apart from injecting triggers to training nodes, these graph backdoor attacks generally require altering the labels of trigger-attached training nodes into the target class, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. In this work, we focus on the clean-label graph backdoor attack, a realistic but understudied topic where training labels are not modifiable. According to our preliminary analysis, existing graph backdoor attacks generally fail under the clean-label setting. Our further analysis identifies that the core failure of existing methods lies in their inability to poison the prediction logic of GNN models, leading to the triggers being deemed unimportant for prediction. Therefore, we study a novel problem of effective clean-label graph backdoor attacks by poisoning the inner prediction logic of GNN models. We propose BA-Logic to solve the problem by coordinating a poisoned node selector and a logic-poisoning trigger generator. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that our method effectively enhances the attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BA-Logic

AI.tldr: BA-Logic: A clean-label graph backdoor attack method that poisons GNN models' inner prediction logic without modifying training labels, achieving effective attacks in realistic scenarios.
AI.motivation: Existing graph backdoor attacks require modifying training labels to inject triggers, which is impractical in real-world scenarios. Clean-label attacks (where training labels are not modifiable) are realistic but understudied, and current methods fail under clean-label settings because they can't poison the GNN's prediction logic.
AI.method: Proposes BA-Logic with a poisoned node selector and logic-poisoning trigger generator that coordinates to poison the inner prediction logic of GNN models. The method works without modifying training labels by making triggers crucial for prediction rather than relying on label changes.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on real-world datasets show BA-Logic effectively enhances attack success rate and surpasses state-of-the-art graph backdoor attack competitors under clean-label settings.
AI.conclusion: BA-Logic successfully addresses the clean-label graph backdoor attack problem by poisoning GNNs' prediction logic rather than relying on label manipulation, making backdoor attacks more practical and effective in real-world scenarios where label modification is impossible.

title: Ailed: A Psyche-Driven Chess Engine with Dynamic Emotional Modulation
comment: 27 pages, 8 figures, 11 tables. Open source: https://github.com/chrnx-dev/ailed-chess
summary:
Chess engines passed human strength years ago, but they still don't play like humans. A grandmaster under clock pressure blunders in ways a club player on a hot streak never would. Conventional engines capture none of this.
  This paper proposes a personality x psyche decomposition to produce behavioral variability in chess play, drawing on patterns observed in human games. Personality is static -- a preset that pins down the engine's character. Psyche is dynamic -- a bounded scalar ψ_t \in [-100, +100], recomputed from five positional factors after every move. These two components feed into an audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, five-band equalizer, saturation limiter) that reshapes move probability distributions on the fly. The chain doesn't care what engine sits behind it: any system that outputs move probabilities will do. It needs no search and carries no state beyond ψ_t.
  I test the framework across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100, feeding it two probability sources that differ by ~2,800x in training data. Both show the same monotonic gradient in top-move agreement (~20-25 pp spread from stress to overconfidence), which tells us the behavioral variation comes from the signal chain, not from the model underneath. When the psyche runs overconfident, the chain mostly gets out of the way (66% agreement with vanilla Maia2). Under stress, the competitive score falls from 50.8% to 30.1%. The patterns are reminiscent of tilt and overconfidence as described in human play, but I should be upfront: this study includes no human-subject validation.

AI.tldr: A signal-chain framework decomposes chess behavior into static personality and dynamic psyche components, reshaping move probabilities to produce human-like behavioral variability without human validation.
AI.motivation: Current chess engines lack human-like behavioral variability - they don't capture the blunders, stress responses, or psychological factors observed in human play under pressure.
AI.method: Decompose behavior into static personality (character preset) and dynamic psyche (ψ_t ∈ [-100,+100] from positional factors). Use audio-inspired signal chain (noise gate, compressor/expander, equalizer, limiter) to reshape move probabilities from any engine without search or state.
AI.result: Tested across 12,414 games against Maia2-1100 with two probability sources (2,800x training data difference). Both show monotonic 20-25pp spread in top-move agreement from stress to overconfidence. Overconfidence: 66% agreement with vanilla engine. Stress: competitive score drops from 50.8% to 30.1%.
AI.conclusion: The signal chain creates behavioral variation independent of underlying model. Patterns resemble human tilt/overconfidence but lack human validation. Framework enables human-like behavioral variability in any chess engine.

title: MCEL: Margin-Based Cross-Entropy Loss for Error-Tolerant Quantized Neural Networks
summary: Robustness to bit errors is a key requirement for the reliable use of neural networks (NNs) on emerging approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. A common approach to achieve bit error tolerance in NNs is injecting bit flips during training according to a predefined error model. While effective in certain scenarios, training-time bit flip injection introduces substantial computational overhead, often degrades inference accuracy at high error rates, and scales poorly for larger NN architectures. These limitations make error injection an increasingly impractical solution for ensuring robustness on future approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies. In this work, we investigate the mechanisms that enable NNs to tolerate bit errors without relying on error-aware training. We establish a direct connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer. Building on this insight, we propose a novel loss function, the Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL), which explicitly promotes logit-level margin separation while preserving the favorable optimization properties of the standard cross-entropy loss. Furthermore, MCEL introduces an interpretable margin parameter that allows robustness to be tuned in a principled manner. Extensive experimental evaluations across multiple datasets of varying complexity, diverse NN architectures, and a range of quantization schemes demonstrate that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance, up to 15 % in accuracy for an error rate of 1 %. Our proposed MCEL method is simple to implement, efficient, and can be integrated as a drop-in replacement for standard CEL. It provides a scalable and principled alternative to training-time bit flip injection, offering new insights into the origins of NN robustness and enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems.

AI.tldr: A novel Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL) method improves neural network bit error tolerance without error injection training, achieving up to 15% accuracy improvement at 1% error rates.
AI.motivation: Current approaches using training-time bit flip injection have substantial computational overhead, degrade inference accuracy at high error rates, and scale poorly for larger neural network architectures, making them impractical for future approximate computing platforms and error-prone memory technologies.
AI.method: The paper establishes a connection between bit error tolerance and classification margins at the output layer, then proposes Margin Cross-Entropy Loss (MCEL) which promotes logit-level margin separation while preserving cross-entropy's optimization properties, with an interpretable margin parameter for principled robustness tuning.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across multiple datasets, diverse neural network architectures, and quantization schemes demonstrate that MCEL substantially improves bit error tolerance, achieving up to 15% accuracy improvement for 1% error rates compared to standard approaches.
AI.conclusion: MCEL provides a simple, efficient, drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy loss that offers scalable and principled bit error tolerance without error injection training, enabling more efficient deployment on approximate computing and memory systems while providing new insights into neural network robustness mechanisms.

title: PACE: A Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine for 9-1-1 Call-taker Training
summary: 9-1-1 call-taking training requires mastery of over a thousand interdependent skills, covering diverse incident types and protocol-specific nuances. A nationwide labor shortage is already straining training capacity, but effective instruction still demands that trainers tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies. This personalization burden is one that current practice cannot scale. Partnering with Metro Nashville Department of Emergency Communications (MNDEC), we propose PACE (Personalized Adaptive Curriculum Engine), a co-pilot system that augments trainer decision-making by (1) maintaining probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, (2) modeling individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and (3) recommending training scenarios that balance acquisition of new competencies with retention of existing ones. PACE propagates evidence over a structured skill graph to accelerate diagnostic coverage and applies contextual bandits to select scenarios that target gaps the trainee is prepared to address. Empirical results show that PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies with practicing training officers further demonstrate a 95.45% alignment rate between PACE's and experts' pedagogical judgments on real-world cases. Under estimation, PACE cuts turnaround time to merely 34 seconds from 11.58 minutes, up to 95.08% reduction.

AI.tldr: PACE is an AI co-pilot system that personalizes 9-1-1 call-taker training using probabilistic skill modeling and adaptive scenario recommendations, achieving significantly faster competency times and higher mastery compared to existing methods.
AI.motivation: 9-1-1 call-taking training involves mastering over 1000 interdependent skills, but nationwide labor shortages strain training capacity. Current practice cannot scale personalized instruction needed to tailor objectives to each trainee's evolving competencies.
AI.method: PACE maintains probabilistic beliefs over trainee skill states, models individual learning and forgetting dynamics, and recommends training scenarios balancing new competency acquisition with existing skill retention. It propagates evidence over a structured skill graph for faster diagnostic coverage and uses contextual bandits to select scenarios targeting gaps the trainee is ready to address.
AI.result: PACE achieves 19.50% faster time-to-competence and 10.95% higher terminal mastery compared to state-of-the-art frameworks. Co-pilot studies show 95.45% alignment with experts' pedagogical judgments, and reduces turnaround time from 11.58 minutes to just 34 seconds (95.08% reduction).
AI.conclusion: PACE demonstrates that AI-driven personalized training systems can effectively augment human trainers, significantly improving training efficiency and quality while reducing burden, with strong potential to address critical workforce shortages in emergency communications.

title: Asymptotic Behavior of Multi--Task Learning: Implicit Regularization and Double Descent Effects
summary: Multi--task learning seeks to improve the generalization error by leveraging the common information shared by multiple related tasks. One challenge in multi--task learning is identifying formulations capable of uncovering the common information shared between different but related tasks. This paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi--task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models. The main contribution of this paper is to precisely determine the reasons behind the benefits gained from combining multiple related tasks. Specifically, we show that combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to a traditional formulation with additional regularization terms that help improve the generalization performance. Another contribution is to empirically study the impact of combining tasks on the generalization error. In particular, we empirically show that the combination of multiple tasks postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.

AI.tldr: Multi-task learning improves generalization by sharing common information between tasks; asymptotic analysis shows task combination acts like additional regularization, postponing/mitigating double descent.
AI.motivation: Multi-task learning aims to improve generalization by leveraging common information across related tasks, but a key challenge is identifying formulations that can effectively uncover this shared information. The paper seeks to understand why and how combining multiple related tasks provides benefits in generalization performance.
AI.method: The paper provides a precise asymptotic analysis of a popular multi-task formulation associated with misspecified perceptron learning models. The analysis mathematically determines how task combination affects learning dynamics and generalization bounds.
AI.result: 1) Combining multiple tasks is asymptotically equivalent to traditional single-task formulation with additional regularization terms that improve generalization. 2) Empirically, combining tasks postpones the double descent phenomenon and can mitigate it asymptotically.
AI.conclusion: The study provides theoretical and empirical evidence that multi-task learning benefits generalization through regularization effects equivalent to additional constraints, and helps manage the double descent phenomenon by effectively leveraging shared information across tasks.

title: Legal interpretation and AI: from expert systems to argumentation and LLMs
summary: AI and Law research has encountered legal interpretation in different ways, in the context of its evolving approaches and methodologies. Research on expert system has focused on legal knowledge engineering, with the goal of ensuring that human-generated interpretations can be precisely transferred into knowledge-bases, to be consistently applied. Research on argumentation has aimed at representing the structure of interpretive arguments, as well as their dialectical interactions, to assess of the acceptability of interpretive claims within argumentation frameworks. Research on machine learning has focused on the automated generation of interpretive suggestions and arguments, through general and specialised language models, now being increasingly deployed in legal practice.

AI.tldr: The paper reviews three main approaches to legal interpretation in AI and Law research: expert systems for knowledge engineering, argumentation systems for analyzing interpretive arguments, and machine learning for generating interpretations.
AI.motivation: The paper examines how AI and Law research has approached the complex problem of legal interpretation as the field has evolved, identifying different methodologies used over time to tackle this challenging aspect of legal reasoning.
AI.method: The paper employs a review and analysis approach, categorizing and examining three distinct research strands: 1) expert systems focusing on legal knowledge engineering, 2) argumentation systems analyzing interpretive argument structures, and 3) machine learning approaches using language models.
AI.result: The analysis identifies three complementary approaches to legal interpretation: knowledge engineering for precise transfer of human interpretations, argumentation frameworks for assessing interpretive claims, and machine learning for automated generation of interpretations now being used in practice.
AI.conclusion: AI and Law research has developed multiple methodologies for legal interpretation, each addressing different aspects of the problem, with machine learning approaches now reaching practical deployment while building on earlier expert systems and argumentation research.

title: Deep Learning-Driven Friendly Jamming for Secure Multicarrier ISAC Under Channel Uncertainty
summary: Integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems promise efficient spectrum utilization by jointly supporting radar sensing and wireless communication. This paper presents a deep learning-driven framework for enhancing physical-layer security in multicarrier ISAC systems under imperfect channel state information (CSI) and in the presence of unknown eavesdropper (Eve) locations. Unlike conventional ISAC-based friendly jamming (FJ) approaches that require Eve's CSI or precise angle-of-arrival (AoA) estimates, our method exploits radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without explicit Eve's information. To enhance robustness to radar sensing uncertainty, we propose a radar-aware neural network that jointly optimizes beamforming and jamming by integrating a novel nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix (FIM) estimator based on f-divergence. The jamming design satisfies the Cramer-Rao lower bound (CRLB) constraints even in the presence of noisy AoA. For efficient implementation, we introduce a quantized tensor train-based encoder that reduces the model size by more than 100 times with negligible performance loss. We also integrate a non-overlapping secure scheme into the proposed framework, in which specific sub-bands can be dedicated solely to communication. Extensive simulations demonstrate that the proposed solution achieves significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced block error rate (BLER), and strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors, underscoring the effectiveness of the proposed deep learning-driven friendly jamming framework under practical ISAC impairments.

AI.tldr: Deep learning framework for secure multicarrier ISAC systems using radar echo feedback for directional jamming without eavesdropper information, with quantization and tensor train compression for efficient implementation.
AI.motivation: Enhance physical-layer security in integrated sensing and communication (ISAC) systems under practical impairments including imperfect channel state information (CSI) and unknown eavesdropper locations, overcoming limitations of conventional approaches that require explicit eavesdropper information.
AI.method: Uses radar echo feedback to guide directional jamming without requiring eavesdropper CSI or AoA estimates. Proposes radar-aware neural network with nonparametric Fisher Information Matrix estimator based on f-divergence to handle sensing uncertainty, and introduces quantized tensor train encoder for model compression. Also integrates non-overlapping secure scheme with dedicated communication sub-bands.
AI.result: Achieves significant improvements in secrecy rate, reduced block error rate, and strong robustness against CSI uncertainty and angular estimation errors. Model compression reduces size by over 100 times with negligible performance loss.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deep learning-driven framework provides an effective solution for enhancing physical-layer security in practical ISAC systems with practical impairments, offering robust performance without requiring explicit eavesdropper information.

title: Judge Reliability Harness: Stress Testing the Reliability of LLM Judges
comment: Accepted at Agents in the Wild: Safety, Security, and Beyond Workshop at ICLR 2026 - April 26, 2026, Rio de Janeiro, Brazil
summary: We present the Judge Reliability Harness, an open source library for constructing validation suites that test the reliability of LLM judges. As LLM based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, more tooling is needed to efficiently assess the reliability of these methods. Given a benchmark dataset and an LLM judge configuration, the harness generates reliability tests that evaluate both binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats. We evaluate four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks spanning safety, persuasion, misuse, and agentic behavior, and find meaningful variation in performance across models and perturbation types, highlighting opportunities to improve the robustness of LLM judges. No judge that we evaluated is uniformly reliable across benchmarks using our harness. For example, our preliminary experiments on judges revealed consistency issues as measured by accuracy in judging another LLM's ability to complete a task due to simple text formatting changes, paraphrasing, changes in verbosity, and flipping the ground truth label in LLM-produced responses. The code for this tool is available at: https://github.com/RANDCorporation/judge-reliability-harness

AI.tldr: A tool called Judge Reliability Harness that evaluates the reliability of LLM judges across various benchmarks and perturbation types, revealing consistency issues in current LLM judging systems.
AI.motivation: LLM-based scoring is widely deployed in AI benchmarks, but there's a lack of tooling to efficiently assess the reliability of these judging methods. Current LLM judges may have hidden consistency issues that need systematic evaluation.
AI.method: Developed an open-source library that generates reliability tests given a benchmark dataset and LLM judge configuration. Tests evaluate binary judgment accuracy and ordinal grading performance for free-response and agentic task formats across various perturbation types.
AI.result: Evaluated four state-of-the-art judges across four benchmarks (safety, persuasion, misuse, agentic behavior) and found meaningful performance variation across models and perturbation types. No judge was uniformly reliable across all benchmarks. Preliminary experiments revealed consistency issues due to text formatting changes, paraphrasing, verbosity changes, and ground truth label flipping.
AI.conclusion: The Judge Reliability Harness is a valuable tool for assessing LLM judge reliability, highlighting opportunities to improve robustness. Current LLM judges show significant reliability variations, indicating the need for more robust evaluation methods before widespread deployment in benchmarks.

title: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
summary: RL agents are typically trained under a single, fixed reward function, which makes them brittle to reward misspecification and limits their ability to adapt to changing task preferences. We introduce Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL), a framework that trains a single agent to optimize a family of reward specifications while collecting experience under only one nominal objective. RCRL conditions the agent on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from a shared replay data entirely off-policy, enabling a single policy to represent reward-specific behaviors. Across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks, we show that RCRL not only improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization, but also enables efficient adaptation to new parameterizations. Our results demonstrate that RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust, steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training.

AI.tldr: RCRL trains a single agent to optimize multiple reward specifications using only one nominal objective's experience, enabling adaptation to different reward parameterizations without multi-task training.
AI.motivation: Current RL agents are brittle to reward misspecification and lack adaptability to changing task preferences because they're typically trained under a single, fixed reward function. This limits their robustness and flexibility in real-world applications.
AI.method: Reward-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning (RCRL) conditions agents on reward parameterizations and learns multiple reward objectives from shared replay data entirely off-policy. The framework trains a single agent using experience collected under only one nominal objective, allowing the policy to represent reward-specific behaviors.
AI.result: RCRL improves performance under the nominal reward parameterization while enabling efficient adaptation to new parameterizations. Results across single-task, multi-task, and vision-based benchmarks demonstrate its effectiveness in learning robust, steerable policies.
AI.conclusion: RCRL provides a scalable mechanism for learning robust, steerable policies without sacrificing the simplicity of single-task training, addressing key limitations of traditional RL approaches regarding reward misspecification and adaptability.

title: Dissociating Direct Access from Inference in AI Introspection
summary: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability, but its mechanism is not well understood. Recent work has shown that AI models can introspect. We study their mechanism of introspection, first extensively replicating Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models. We show that these models detect injected representations via two separable mechanisms: (i) probability-matching (inferring from perceived anomaly of the prompt) and (ii) direct access to internal states. The direct access mechanism is content-agnostic: models detect that an anomaly occurred but cannot reliably identify its semantic content. The two model classes we study confabulate injected concepts that are high-frequency and concrete (e.g., "apple'"); for them correct concept guesses typically require significantly more tokens. This content-agnostic introspective mechanism is consistent with leading theories in philosophy and psychology.

AI.tldr: AI models use two separable mechanisms for introspection: probability-matching (inferring from prompt anomalies) and content-agnostic direct access to internal states, with correct concept identification requiring more tokens and aligning with philosophical/psychological theories.
AI.motivation: Introspection is a foundational cognitive ability with poorly understood mechanisms, and while recent work shows AI models can introspect, their specific introspection mechanisms need investigation.
AI.method: Extensive replication of Lindsey et al. (2025)'s thought injection detection paradigm in large open-source models, analyzing how models detect injected representations through separable mechanisms.
AI.result: Models detect injected representations via two mechanisms: (1) probability-matching (inferring from prompt anomalies) and (2) direct access to internal states (content-agnostic detection). Models confabulate high-frequency, concrete concepts like "apple," and correct concept identification typically requires significantly more tokens.
AI.conclusion: The content-agnostic introspective mechanism (detecting anomalies without semantic content identification) aligns with leading theories in philosophy and psychology, providing insight into AI introspection mechanisms.

title: Synchronization-based clustering on the unit hypersphere
summary: Clustering on the unit hypersphere is a fundamental problem in various fields, with applications ranging from gene expression analysis to text and image classification. Traditional clustering methods are not always suitable for unit sphere data, as they do not account for the geometric structure of the sphere. We introduce a novel algorithm for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere $\mathbf{S}^{d-1}$. Our method is based on the $d$-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model. The effectiveness of the introduced method is demonstrated on synthetic and real-world datasets. Results are compared with some of the traditional clustering methods, showing that our method achieves similar or better results in terms of clustering accuracy.

AI.tldr: A novel clustering algorithm for unit hypersphere data based on the generalized Kuramoto model, achieving competitive performance with traditional methods.
AI.motivation: Traditional clustering methods are inadequate for unit sphere data as they don't account for the geometric structure of the sphere. Clustering on the unit hypersphere is fundamental in many fields including gene expression analysis, text classification, and image classification.
AI.method: The method introduces a novel algorithm for clustering data represented as points on the unit sphere S^{d-1}, based on the d-dimensional generalized Kuramoto model.
AI.result: The method demonstrates effectiveness on both synthetic and real-world datasets. Comparison with traditional clustering methods shows that the proposed method achieves similar or better results in terms of clustering accuracy.
AI.conclusion: The proposed Kuramoto model-based clustering algorithm provides an effective solution for spherical data clustering, addressing the limitations of traditional methods that don't consider spherical geometry.

title: Distributed Partial Information Puzzles: Examining Common Ground Construction Under Epistemic Asymmetry
summary: Establishing common ground, a shared set of beliefs and mutually recognized facts, is fundamental to collaboration, yet remains a challenge for current AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings, where the collaborators bring different information to the table. We introduce the Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP), a collaborative construction task that elicits rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. We present a multimodal dataset of these interactions, annotated and temporally aligned across speech, gesture, and action modalities to support reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics. We then evaluate two paradigms for modeling common ground (CG): (1) state-of-the-art large language models (LLMs), prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline grounded in Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) that incrementally performs the same task. Results on the annotated DPIP data indicate that it poses a challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DPIP, a collaborative construction task for studying multimodal communication under information asymmetry, collects a multimodal dataset, and compares LLMs against a logic-based approach for modeling common ground.
AI.motivation: Establishing common ground (shared beliefs) is crucial for collaboration but challenging for AI systems, especially in multimodal, multiparty settings where participants have different information. There's a need to understand and model how humans establish common ground in such complex scenarios.
AI.method: Introduced Distributed Partial Information Puzzle (DPIP) to elicit rich multimodal communication under epistemic asymmetry. Created a multimodal dataset with temporal alignment across speech, gesture, and action modalities. Evaluated two approaches: (1) state-of-the-art LLMs prompted to infer shared beliefs from multimodal updates, and (2) an axiomatic pipeline based on Dynamic Epistemic Logic (DEL) for incremental belief tracking.
AI.result: DPIP poses a significant challenge to modern LLMs' abilities to track both task progression and belief state. The annotated dataset supports reasoning over propositional content and belief dynamics in multimodal collaborative settings.
AI.conclusion: Current LLMs struggle with tracking common ground in complex multimodal collaborative tasks, highlighting the need for improved AI systems capable of handling epistemic reasoning in information-asymmetric environments. The DPIP framework and dataset provide valuable resources for advancing research in this area.

title: Aura: Universal Multi-dimensional Exogenous Integration for Aviation Time Series
summary: Time series forecasting has witnessed an increasing demand across diverse industrial applications, where accurate predictions are pivotal for informed decision-making. Beyond numerical time series data, reliable forecasting in practical scenarios requires integrating diverse exogenous factors. Such exogenous information is often multi-dimensional or even multimodal, introducing heterogeneous interactions that unimodal time series models struggle to capture. In this paper, we delve into an aviation maintenance scenario and identify three distinct types of exogenous factors that influence temporal dynamics through distinct interaction modes. Based on this empirical insight, we propose Aura, a universal framework that explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information according to its interaction mode with the target time series. Specifically, Aura utilizes a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into well-established time series models, ensuring seamless integration of non-sequential context. Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines, covering the Boeing 777 and Airbus A320 fleets, demonstrate that Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability. Our findings highlight Aura's potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability.

AI.tldr: Aura is a universal framework that integrates heterogeneous exogenous factors into time series forecasting using a tripartite encoding mechanism, validated on large-scale aviation maintenance data.
AI.motivation: Practical time series forecasting needs to integrate diverse exogenous factors beyond numerical data, but current unimodal models struggle with heterogeneous multi-dimensional/multimodal interactions found in real-world scenarios like aviation maintenance.
AI.method: Aura framework explicitly organizes and encodes heterogeneous external information based on interaction modes with target time series. Uses a tailored tripartite encoding mechanism to embed heterogeneous features into established time series models, enabling seamless integration of non-sequential context.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on a large-scale, three-year industrial dataset from China Southern Airlines (Boeing 777 & Airbus A320 fleets) show Aura consistently achieves state-of-the-art performance across all baselines and exhibits superior adaptability.
AI.conclusion: Aura demonstrates strong potential as a general-purpose enhancement for aviation safety and reliability by effectively integrating heterogeneous exogenous factors into time series forecasting models.

title: Towards Provably Unbiased LLM Judges via Bias-Bounded Evaluation
summary: As AI models progress beyond simple chatbots into more complex workflows, we draw ever closer to the event horizon beyond which AI systems will be utilized in autonomous, self-maintaining feedback loops. Any autonomous AI system will depend on automated, verifiable rewards and feedback; in settings where ground truth is sparse or non-deterministic, one practical source of such rewards is an LLM-as-a-Judge. Although LLM judges continue to improve, the literature has yet to introduce systems capable of enforcing standards with strong guarantees, particularly when bias vectors are unknown or adversarially discovered. To remedy this issue, we propose average bias-boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework which formally guarantees reductions of harm/impact as a result of any measurable bias in an LLM judge. Evaluating on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges, we achieve (tau=0.5, delta=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings, with most judge-bias combinations exceeding 80%. The code to reproduce our findings is available at https://github.com/penfever/bias-bounded-evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), a framework that provides formal guarantees for reducing bias in LLM judges used for automated AI system feedback, maintaining high correlation with original rankings while bounding potential harm from bias.
AI.motivation: As AI systems become more autonomous and require automated feedback mechanisms, LLM-as-a-Judge approaches become crucial but lack formal guarantees against bias, especially when biases are unknown or adversarial. Existing systems don't adequately address this safety concern.
AI.method: The authors propose Average Bias-Boundedness (A-BB), an algorithmic framework that formally guarantees reductions in harm/impact resulting from any measurable bias in LLM judges. It provides statistical guarantees on bias bounding while maintaining correlation with original rankings.
AI.result: Evaluation on Arena-Hard-Auto with four LLM judges achieved (τ=0.5, δ=0.01) bias-bounded guarantees while retaining 61-99% correlation with original rankings across formatting and schematic bias settings. Most judge-bias combinations exceeded 80% correlation.
AI.conclusion: The A-BB framework successfully addresses the critical safety gap in LLM judge systems by providing formal bias guarantees while maintaining practical utility, enabling safer deployment of autonomous AI systems that rely on automated feedback loops.

title: Axiomatic On-Manifold Shapley via Optimal Generative Flows
summary: Shapley-based attribution is critical for post-hoc XAI but suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baselines. While generative methods attempt to address this, they often introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift. We propose a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions driven by optimal generative flows. We prove a representation theorem establishing the gradient line integral as the unique functional satisfying efficiency and geometric axioms, notably reparameterization invariance. To resolve path ambiguity, we select the kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic transporting a prior to the data distribution. This yields a canonical attribution family that recovers classical Shapley for additive models and admits provable stability bounds against flow approximation errors. By reframing baseline selection as a variational problem, our method experimentally outperforms baselines, achieving strict manifold adherence via vanishing Flow Consistency Error and superior semantic alignment characterized by Structure-Aware Total Variation. Our code is on https://github.com/cenweizhang/OTFlowSHAP.

AI.tldr: Proposes optimal transport flow-based Shapley attributions that solve off-manifold issues by using Wasserstein-2 geodesics as canonical attribution paths, providing formal guarantees and improved performance.
AI.motivation: Traditional Shapley attribution suffers from off-manifold artifacts due to heuristic baseline selection, while existing generative methods introduce geometric inefficiency and discretization drift.
AI.method: Develops a formal theory of on-manifold Aumann-Shapley attributions using optimal generative flows, proves representation theorem for gradient line integrals, selects kinetic-energy-minimizing Wasserstein-2 geodesic between prior and data distribution as canonical attribution path.
AI.result: Achieves strict manifold adherence via vanishing Flow Consistency Error, superior semantic alignment characterized by Structure-Aware Total Variation, outperforms baselines experimentally, recovers classical Shapley for additive models with provable stability bounds.
AI.conclusion: Reframing baseline selection as a variational problem using optimal transport geodesics provides a principled solution to off-manifold attribution issues with formal guarantees and practical benefits for interpretable machine learning.

title: The Spike, the Sparse and the Sink: Anatomy of Massive Activations and Attention Sinks
summary: We study two recurring phenomena in Transformer language models: massive activations, in which a small number of tokens exhibit extreme outliers in a few channels, and attention sinks, in which certain tokens attract disproportionate attention mass regardless of semantic relevance. Prior work observes that these phenomena frequently co-occur and often involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Through systematic experiments, we show that the co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact of modern Transformer design, and that the two phenomena serve related but distinct functions. Massive activations operate globally: they induce near-constant hidden representations that persist across layers, effectively functioning as implicit parameters of the model. Attention sinks operate locally: they modulate attention outputs across heads and bias individual heads toward short-range dependencies. We identify the pre-norm configuration as the key choice that enables the co-occurrence, and show that ablating it causes the two phenomena to decouple.

AI.tldr: Transformer models exhibit two co-occurring phenomena: massive activations (few tokens have extreme outliers in channels) and attention sinks (certain tokens attract disproportionate attention). The study shows these are architectural artifacts of modern Transformer design with distinct functions.
AI.motivation: Prior work observed that massive activations and attention sinks frequently co-occur and involve the same tokens, but their functional roles and causal relationship remain unclear. Understanding these phenomena is important as they're recurring patterns in Transformer language models that may affect model behavior.
AI.method: Conducted systematic experiments to investigate the relationship between massive activations and attention sinks. The key approach involved analyzing the pre-norm configuration as the architectural element enabling their co-occurrence, and performing ablation studies where removing pre-norm causes the phenomena to decouple.
AI.result: Found that massive activations operate globally by inducing near-constant hidden representations across layers, effectively functioning as implicit model parameters. Attention sinks operate locally by modulating attention outputs across heads and biasing individual heads toward short-range dependencies. The pre-norm configuration is identified as the key architectural choice enabling their co-occurrence.
AI.conclusion: Massive activations and attention sinks serve related but distinct functions in Transformer models, and their co-occurrence is largely an architectural artifact rather than an intrinsic functional coupling. The study provides clearer understanding of these phenomena's roles and demonstrates how architectural choices (pre-norm) influence model behavior patterns.

title: Decoupling Task and Behavior: A Two-Stage Reward Curriculum in Reinforcement Learning for Robotics
summary: Deep Reinforcement Learning is a promising tool for robotic control, yet practical application is often hindered by the difficulty of designing effective reward functions. Real-world tasks typically require optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously, necessitating precise tuning of their weights to learn a policy with the desired characteristics. To address this, we propose a two-stage reward curriculum where we decouple task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. In our method, we first train the agent on a simplified task-only reward function to ensure effective exploration before introducing the full reward that includes auxiliary behavior-related terms such as energy efficiency. Further, we analyze various transition strategies and demonstrate that reusing samples between phases is critical for training stability. We validate our approach on the DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and a mobile robot environment, modified to include auxiliary behavioral objectives. Our method proves to be simple yet effective, substantially outperforming baselines trained directly on the full reward while exhibiting higher robustness to specific reward weightings.

AI.tldr: Two-stage reward curriculum method decouples task objectives from behavioral terms, first training on simplified task rewards before adding auxiliary behavioral objectives, outperforming direct training on full rewards.
AI.motivation: Deep reinforcement learning for robotic control is hindered by difficulty designing effective reward functions, especially when tasks require optimizing multiple objectives simultaneously. Precise tuning of reward weights is needed to achieve desired policy characteristics, which is challenging in practice.
AI.method: Propose a two-stage reward curriculum that decouples task-specific objectives from behavioral terms. First train agent on simplified task-only reward function to ensure effective exploration, then introduce full reward including auxiliary behavior-related terms (like energy efficiency). Also analyze various transition strategies and demonstrate importance of reusing samples between phases for training stability.
AI.result: Method validated on DeepMind Control Suite, ManiSkill3, and mobile robot environment modified to include auxiliary behavioral objectives. Substantially outperforms baselines trained directly on full reward, exhibiting higher robustness to specific reward weightings.
AI.conclusion: The two-stage reward curriculum approach is simple yet effective for handling multi-objective robotic control tasks, providing better performance and robustness than direct training on complex reward functions.

title: FedBCD:Communication-Efficient Accelerated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent for Federated Learning
summary:
Although Federated Learning has been widely studied in recent years, there are still high overhead expenses in each communication round for large-scale models such as Vision Transformer. To lower the communication complexity, we propose a novel Federated Block Coordinate Gradient Descent (FedBCGD) method for communication efficiency. The proposed method splits model parameters into several blocks, including a shared block and enables uploading a specific parameter block by each client, which can significantly reduce communication overhead. Moreover, we also develop an accelerated FedBCGD algorithm (called FedBCGD+) with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction. To the best of our knowledge, this paper is the first work on parameter block communication for training large-scale deep models. We also provide the convergence analysis for the proposed algorithms. Our theoretical results show that the communication complexities of our algorithms are a factor $1/N$ lower than those of existing methods, where $N$ is the number of parameter blocks, and they enjoy much faster convergence than their counterparts. Empirical results indicate the superiority of the proposed algorithms compared to state-of-the-art algorithms.
  The code is available at https://github.com/junkangLiu0/FedBCGD.

AI.tldr: FedBCGD reduces communication costs in federated learning for large models by splitting parameters into blocks and having clients upload specific blocks instead of full models.
AI.motivation: Federated learning for large-scale models like Vision Transformers incurs high communication overhead in each round, which needs to be reduced.
AI.method: FedBCGD splits model parameters into blocks (including a shared block) and enables each client to upload only a specific parameter block. Also developed FedBCGD+ with client drift control and stochastic variance reduction.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows communication complexities reduced by factor 1/N (N = number of blocks) with faster convergence. Empirical results show superiority over state-of-the-art methods.
AI.conclusion: FedBCGD is the first parameter block communication approach for large-scale deep models, significantly reducing communication overhead while maintaining or improving convergence.

title: Federated Causal Discovery Across Heterogeneous Datasets under Latent Confounding
summary: Causal discovery across multiple datasets is often constrained by data privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity, limiting the use of conventional methods that require a single, centralized dataset. To address these challenges, we introduce fedCI, a federated conditional independence test that rigorously handles heterogeneous datasets with non-identical sets of variables, site-specific effects, and mixed variable types, including continuous, ordinal, binary, and categorical variables. At its core, fedCI uses a federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) procedure to estimate the parameters of generalized linear models underlying likelihood-ratio tests for conditional independence. Building on this, we develop fedCI-IOD, a federated extension of the Integration of Overlapping Datasets (IOD) algorithm, that replaces its meta-analysis strategy and enables, for the fist time, federated causal discovery under latent confounding across distributed and heterogeneous datasets. By aggregating evidence federatively, fedCI-IOD not only preserves privacy but also substantially enhances statistical power, achieving performance comparable to fully pooled analyses and mitigating artifacts from low local sample sizes. Our tools are publicly available as the fedCI Python package, a privacy-preserving R implementation of IOD, and a web application for the fedCI-IOD pipeline, providing versatile, user-friendly solutions for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery.

AI.tldr: fedCI enables federated conditional independence testing across heterogeneous datasets with privacy preservation, and fedCI-IOD extends this for federated causal discovery under latent confounding.
AI.motivation: Causal discovery across multiple datasets is constrained by data privacy regulations and cross-site heterogeneity, preventing use of centralized conventional methods due to non-identical variable sets, site-specific effects, and mixed data types.
AI.method: Introduces fedCI using federated Iteratively Reweighted Least Squares (IRLS) to estimate parameters for generalized linear models underlying likelihood-ratio tests. Extends to fedCI-IOD, a federated version of IOD algorithm that replaces meta-analysis with federated evidence aggregation.
AI.result: fedCI-IOD preserves privacy while substantially enhancing statistical power, achieving performance comparable to fully pooled analyses and mitigating low local sample size artifacts.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents fedCI as a versatile solution for federated conditional independence testing and causal discovery, providing Python/R implementations and a web application for user-friendly federated analysis while addressing privacy and heterogeneity challenges.

title: Balancing Privacy-Quality-Efficiency in Federated Learning through Round-Based Interleaving of Protection Techniques
summary:
In federated learning (FL), balancing privacy protection, learning quality, and efficiency remains a challenge. Privacy protection mechanisms, such as Differential Privacy (DP), degrade learning quality, or, as in the case of Homomorphic Encryption (HE), incur substantial system overhead. To address this, we propose Alt-FL, a privacy-preserving FL framework that combines DP, HE, and synthetic data via a novel round-based interleaving strategy. Alt-FL introduces three new methods, Privacy Interleaving (PI), Synthetic Interleaving with DP (SI/DP), and Synthetic Interleaving with HE (SI/HE), that enable flexible quality-efficiency trade-offs while providing privacy protection.
